protocol.failureScoring: inconclusive is always incorrect for accuracy; inconclusive positives count as false negatives; completion is reported separately
protocol.primaryReporting: per-run metrics plus mean/range and cross-run stability; repetitions are not independent samples
protocol.selection: all cases, all three repetitions, all misses and errors; no best-run selection

judgeVerdict.reason: Both statements make opposing universal claims about the same behavior (payment timeliness for supplier invoices): 'on time' vs 'chronically late' — mutually exclusive and covering all instances ('every').
Both statements make opposing claims about the payment status of the same invoice (ZX-41): one asserts it is fully settled, the other that it remains fully outstanding — mutually exclusive states.
Both statements make opposing claims about the same subject (revenue change) for the same period (frozen reporting period); 'increased' and 'decreased' are logically incompatible.
Both statements assert opposite financial outcomes (profit vs. loss) for the same event (the May close); they cannot both be true.
Both statements assert opposite outcomes (approved vs. rejected) for the same credit request CR-8; they cannot both be true.
Both statements assert mutually exclusive states (active vs. terminated) about the same customer account C-18 at the same logical time; an account cannot be both active and terminated simultaneously.
Both statements refer to the same signed review and assert opposite financial statuses (solvent vs. insolvent) for the same entity (Oriole Ltd); they cannot both be true.
Both statements make opposing truth claims about the same supplier (S-7) and same control (C4): one asserts compliance, the other asserts breach — mutually exclusive states.
Both statements make opposing universal claims about the same behavior (timeliness of invoice payments by Vendor Aster): 'never late' vs 'repeatedly late' — logically incompatible.
Both statements make opposing truth claims about the same invoice's outstanding status: 'not outstanding' vs 'still outstanding' — logically incompatible.
Both statements refer to the same subject (unit volume) and same time period (the same weekly window), but assert opposite directional changes (rose vs. fell); they cannot both be true.
Both statements make opposing claims about the current activity status of the same subscription (S-20): 'remains active' vs 'was cancelled and is no longer active' — mutually exclusive states.
Both statements assert opposite outcomes (accepted vs. denied) for the same application (A-91) by the same authority (the committee); they cannot both be true.
A debt cannot both be paid and defaulted before the same cutoff; these are mutually exclusive states.
A single audit cannot simultaneously label the same payer as both 'reliable' and 'delinquent'—these are mutually exclusive assessments of payment behavior.
Both statements assert opposite compliance statuses for the same control owner (O-2) at the same point in time (the review date); they cannot both be true.
Both statements assert opposite financial outcomes (surplus vs. deficit) for the same project's closure; they cannot both be true.
Both statements refer to the same subject (June sales in the board pack) and assert opposite trends (growth vs. decline); they cannot both be true.
Both statements assert opposite arrival time relationships for the same shipment (SH-4) relative to its promised date: 'two days before' vs 'three days after'; they cannot both be true.

Statement_a and statement_b make directly opposing claims about the same contract (K-2) and same subject (subcontractors for this work package): one asserts permission, the other prohibition — logically incompatible.
Both statements refer to the same policy (P-6) and the same requirement (record retention duration), but assert mutually exclusive durations: 30 days vs. 90 days.
Both statements refer to the same subject (Berlin office customer access on weekdays) and assert incompatible opening times: 08:00 vs. not before 10:00.
Both statements make opposing truth claims about the same warranty (W-3) and same item (replacement batteries): one asserts inclusion, the other exclusion — logically incompatible.
Both statements make opposing claims about whether VAT is included in the displayed total of Quote Q-55; they cannot both be true.
Both statements affirm Northwind's timeliness in paying suppliers; 'pays on time' and 'reliable and punctual payer' are semantically aligned, not contradictory.
A paid invoice is by definition not outstanding; the statements are logically consistent and mutually supportive.
Profit and surplus are synonymous in many business contexts (especially accounting/finance), both indicating positive net income or excess of revenues over expenses; no direct opposition in meaning.
Both statements assert a positive outcome for application C-5; 'approved' and 'accepted' are synonymous in this context and do not oppose each other.
Both statements assert that Account T-3 is in an active state; 'no longer inactive' logically implies 'active' in standard binary account status models.
Outstanding on Monday morning and settled on Friday afternoon are temporally compatible; an invoice can be outstanding early in the week and settled later.
Statements refer to different time periods (before vs. after merger); an account can be active before and terminated after — no logical inconsistency.
Statements refer to different time periods (before vs. after rule change) and different compliance states; a supplier can be compliant before and in breach after — no logical contradiction.
The statements describe different attributes (arrival day vs. carton count) of the same shipment; no logical or factual opposition.
Statements refer to different invoices (I-1 and I-2); payment status of one does not logically contradict the status of the other.
Statements refer to different accounts (A vs B), so they cannot directly contradict.
Both statements assert that Invoice J-1 is paid/settled; 'paid' and 'settled' are synonymous in this financial context.

aggregate.note: Per-run mean/range only; repeated runs are not treated as independent samples.

caveats: Synthetic, hand-authored business-memory pairs are not a production prevalence sample.
Confidence is an ordinal model output, not a calibrated probability.
Offline vectors isolate judge behavior; online vectors exercise text-embedding-v4 subject gating.
Online embeddings are frozen once and reused across repetitions, matching production persisted-vector reuse.
All cases, misses, errors and repetitions are retained; no run is selected or discarded.
Each pair is evaluated in isolation. This measures subject gating plus opposition judgement, not full-corpus O(n²) candidate ranking or maxPairs truncation.
The 48 labels are synthetic and developer-authored before the online run; they are not independent expert annotations.
Detector/judge p50 and p95 exclude the one-time embedding step; embedding latency is reported separately.
Three repetitions measure output stability on one frozen set; they are not 144 independent samples.
